title: FHA 203k Loan Florida 2026 | Renovation Mortgage Guide
description: FHA 203(k) renovation loans in Florida let you finance a home purchase plus repairs in one loan. Learn the Limited vs Standard 203(k), eligible improvements, and how it works.

bodyHtml:
Home/FHA 203k Loan Florida
FHA 203k Loan Florida

FHA 203(k) renovation loans in Florida

Buy a Florida fixer-upper and finance the renovations in one loan. Here's how FHA 203(k) loans work, what they cover, and the difference between Limited and Standard.

Get My Free Rate QuoteCall __PHONE_DISPLAY__
Buy + renovateLimited & StandardLow FHA downOne closing

Get your free FHA quote

No obligation. See your rate & payment in minutes.

🔒 Your information is secure. By submitting you agree to be contacted about FHA loan options. This is not a commitment to lend.

Thank you!

An FHA mortgage specialist will reach out shortly with your personalized Florida rate quote.

Renovation Loans

FHA 203(k) renovation loans in Florida

An FHA 203(k) loan lets you finance the purchase of a home and the cost of renovating it in a single loan with one closing — still with the low FHA down payment. It is ideal for Florida fixer-uppers, older homes, and houses that need work to pass an FHA appraisal. Instead of buying, then scrambling for a separate renovation loan, you wrap it all into one mortgage based on the home’s value after the improvements.

Down payment3.5%

On total project cost

Limited 203(k)Up to $75,000

In repairs

StructureOne loan

Purchase + reno, one closing

Min score580

Standard FHA credit rules

Limited vs. Standard

Two types of 203(k)

There are two versions of the 203(k), and choosing the right one comes down to the size and nature of the work:

Limited 203(k)Standard 203(k)
Max repair costUp to $75,000No set cap (within FHA loan limit)
Structural workNot allowedAllowed (additions, foundation)
HUD consultantNot requiredRequired
Repair timelineUp to 9 monthsUp to 12 months
Typical useKitchens, baths, roof, systems, cosmeticsMajor rehabs, room additions, code work

The Limited 203(k) cap rose to $75,000 for case numbers assigned on or after November 4, 2024 — a big jump from the old $35,000, which now covers a lot more of a real Florida renovation.

Coverage

What you can finance

What it can cover

  • Roofing, windows, siding, and gutters — common after Florida storm seasons.
  • HVAC, plumbing, and electrical systems.
  • Kitchen and bath remodels, flooring, and paint.
  • Accessibility upgrades.
  • Structural work, room additions, and foundation repair (Standard 203(k) only).
  • An eligible accessory dwelling unit in some cases.

What it won’t cover

Luxury items that don’t add permanent value — think outdoor pools (as a new luxury add), or anything you could take with you. The work must be done by a licensed general contractor; DIY projects are not eligible.

How the loan amount is figured

Your loan is based on the lesser of (a) the purchase price plus renovation costs, or (b) 110% of the home’s appraised value after the improvements — and the total must stay within the FHA loan limit for your county (the 2026 Florida floor is $541,287, higher in places like Monroe County and the high-cost metros).

Example

Putting it together

A simplified Florida example — buying a $280,000 home that needs $55,000 in work:

Purchase + reno$335,000

Total project cost

3.5% down$11,725

On the total

Loan amount$328,932

Incl. financed upfront MIP

Est. payment$2,677/mo

P&I + MIP + tax + ins

Illustration only, using a sample rate for demonstration. Rates, taxes, insurance, and mortgage insurance change and vary by loan, county, and borrower — your actual numbers will differ. Not a commitment to lend.

Note that 203(k) rates typically run about 0.75–1.0% higher than a standard FHA loan, and Standard 203(k) projects include a contingency reserve and a HUD consultant fee. We’ll lay out the full picture before you commit.

How It Works

Buying and renovating in one loan

01

Find the home

Identify a property that needs work. We get you pre-approved for a 203(k) so you can make a confident offer.

02

Scope the work

Get contractor bids. A Standard 203(k) brings in a HUD consultant to document the scope and oversee the project.

03

"As-completed" appraisal

The appraiser estimates the home’s value after the planned improvements, which sets your loan amount.

04

Close, then renovate

You close on the combined loan; renovation funds are released to your contractor in draws as the work is completed and inspected.

Questions & Answers

Frequently asked questions

What is an FHA 203(k) loan?+
A renovation mortgage that combines the purchase (or refinance) of a home and the cost of eligible repairs or improvements into a single FHA loan with one closing.
What’s the difference between Limited and Standard 203(k)?+
The Limited 203(k) covers non-structural repairs up to $75,000 and needs no HUD consultant. The Standard 203(k) handles larger or structural projects with no set repair cap (within your FHA loan limit) and requires a HUD consultant to oversee the work.
How much can I borrow for repairs?+
Under the Limited 203(k), up to $75,000 in repair costs (for case numbers assigned on or after November 4, 2024). The Standard 203(k) has no fixed repair cap, but your total loan must stay within the FHA loan limit for your county.
What can a 203(k) pay for?+
Roofing, HVAC, plumbing and electrical, kitchens and baths, flooring, windows, siding, accessibility upgrades, and — under the Standard program — structural work and room additions. Luxury items like a new pool generally aren’t eligible, and the work must be done by a licensed contractor.
How is the loan amount calculated?+
It’s based on the lesser of the purchase price plus renovation costs, or 110% of the home’s appraised value after the improvements, and it must fall within your county’s FHA loan limit.
Can I do the work myself?+
No. The 203(k) requires a licensed general contractor. DIY projects are not eligible.
Can I use a 203(k) to renovate my current home?+
Yes — a 203(k) can be used to refinance and renovate a home you already own, not just for purchases, as long as the repairs total at least $5,000.
How long does the renovation have to take?+
Up to nine months for a Limited 203(k) and up to twelve months for a Standard 203(k).
